---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
---

# 

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(tidyquant)
library(plotly)

# 1. Map state codes for the Southwest Region
southwest_states <- tibble(
  state_code = c("TX", "OK", "NM", "AZ"),
  state_name = c("Texas", "Oklahoma", "New Mexico", "Arizona")
)

# 2. Programmatically fetch live monthly Unemployment Rates for each state
# FRED state codes use the pattern: [STATE]UR (e.g., TXUR)
sw_unemployment <- southwest_states$state_code %>% 
  map_df(~ {
    tq_get(paste0(.x, "UR"), get = "economic.data", from = "2005-01-01") %>% 
      mutate(state_code = .x)
  }) %>% 
  rename(unemployment_rate = price)

# 3. Pull Regional Consumer Price Index (CPI) to calculate annualized inflation
# FRED uses the Census South regional marker (CUUR0300SA0) for broad southern indexing
sw_inflation <- tq_get("CUUR0300SA0", get = "economic.data", from = "2004-01-01") %>% 
  mutate(
    inflation_rate = (price - lag(price, 12)) / lag(price, 12) * 100
  ) %>% 
  filter(!is.na(inflation_rate)) %>% 
  select(date, inflation_rate)

# 4. Join datasets and structure modern chronological clusters
southwest_data <- sw_unemployment %>% 
  left_join(sw_inflation, by = "date") %>% 
  left_join(southwest_states, by = "state_code") %>% 
  filter(!is.na(inflation_rate)) %>% 
  mutate(
    year = as.numeric(format(date, "%Y")),
    era = case_when(
      year %in% 2005:2007 ~ "Pre-Financial Crisis",
      year %in% 2008:2015 ~ "Post-Great Recession",
      year %in% 2016:2019 ~ "Pre-Pandemic Expansion",
      year %in% 2020:2022 ~ "Pandemic Inflation Shock",
      TRUE ~ "Current Recovery & Stability"
    ),
    period_label = format(date, "%B %Y")
  )

# 5. Formulate the aesthetic foundation split by Southwest State
sw_plot <- ggplot(southwest_data, aes(
  x = unemployment_rate, 
  y = inflation_rate, 
  color = era,
  text = paste("State:", state_name, "<br>Period:", period_label, "<br>Unemployment:", round(unemployment_rate, 1), "%<br>Inflation:", round(inflation_rate, 1), "%")
)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.65, size = 2.5) +
  facet_wrap(~state_name, scales = "free_x") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  labs(
    title = "Southwest Regional Phillips Curve (2005 - Present)",
    x = "State Unemployment Rate (%)",
    y = "Regional Inflation Rate (% Change Year-Over-Year)"
  ) +
  theme(
    legend.title = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# 6. Render the interactive panel layout in the Notebook
#   ggplotly(sw_plot, tooltip = "text")

# Save plot as a .htlml pbject
# 6. Assign the interactive panel layout to a variable
#interactive_sw_plot <- ggplotly(sw_plot, tooltip = "text")

# 7. Save the interactive chart as a standalone HTML file
# If you don't have the htmlwidgets package, run: install.packages("htmlwidgets")
#library(htmlwidgets)
#saveWidget(interactive_sw_plot, "southwest_phillips_curve.html", selfcontained = TRUE)

# 8. Still display the plot inside your RStudio notebook
#interactive_sw_plot

# Save as a compressed file
# 6. Assign the interactive panel layout to a variable
interactive_sw_plot <- ggplotly(sw_plot, tooltip = "text")

# 7. HIGHLY COMPRESSED LOCAL EXPORT
# Using htmltools dependency bundling drastically reduces standalone file footprint
library(htmlwidgets)
library(htmltools)

saveWidget(
  widget = interactive_sw_plot, 
  file = "southwest_phillips_curve.html", 
  selfcontained = FALSE,          # Triggers maximum compression by separating assets
  libdir = "lib"                  # Pools the shared JavaScript code into an external folder
)

# 8. AUTOSAVE / DEPLOY DIRECTLY TO YOUR ONLINE POSIT ACCOUNT
# This instantly pushes the interactive asset to your cloud workspace dashboard
# If rsconnect is not yet configured, run: install.packages("rsconnect")
library(rsconnect)

# Note: The first time you use this, click the 'Publish' button 
# (blue curling icon in the top right of your RStudio script window) 
# to link your Posit Cloud or Posit Connect account token.
rsconnect::deployDoc(
  doc = "southwest_phillips_curve.html",
  appName = "southwest-regional-economics",
  account = rsconnect::accounts()$name[1] # Automatically selects your active Posit profile
)

# 9. Print inside notebook
#interactive_sw_plot



```
